senses.gloss: until, as far as, to the point of, even up to

examples.english: I ate the fish, until its head
The people went forth, even the children

examples.text: كُلُّ تَعْمِيمٍ بَاطِلٌ، حَتَّى هَذَا التَّعْمِيمُ.
examples.english: All generalizations are false, including this one.

senses.gloss: even, even this, including, even if

etymology.text:
* Traditionally, Arabic grammarians link it to the root ح ت ت (ḥ t t) in the senses of "to scrape or rub off", "to remove or destroy", "to end something", "to drive back or repel"; hence the meaning of an end-limit, the ending barrier or the point that holds back. Such a derivation would be highly irregular.
* Some classical dialects attest the word as عَتَّى (ʕattā), which is closer to the usual Semitic form found in Ugaritic 𐎓𐎄 (ʿd), Hebrew עַד (ʿaḏ), Aramaic עַד (ʿaḏ), and Akkadian 𒀀𒁲 (adi). If this is related, it could be connected with the root ع ت ي (ʕ t y), meaning "to be excessive", "to push the limits or be at the threshold", "to be extremely old or at the extreme end of life".
* Al-Jallad proposes an etymology from حَدّ (ḥadd, “limit”) + a conjunction *tay, as seen in مَتَى (matā, “when”, literally “what + *tay”) and North Levantine Arabic تَـ (ta-).

body.text: is a particle, used in three senses: (Mughnee:) it is a particle denoting the end of an extent; (Mughnee, K;) which is its predominant meaning; (Mughnee;) asserted by some to be always its meaning: (TA:) and denoting a cause, or motive: and syn. with
as an exceptive; (Mughnee, K;) which last is the rarest, and is mentioned by few. (Mughnee.) ―         -b2-  It is used as a preposition governing the gen. case, in the same manner as
(S, Mughnee) in respect of meaning and government, (Mughnee,) denoting the end of an extent; (S;) [signifying

] but the word that it so governs must be a noun properly so called, not a pronoun except in a case of poetic license; and must signify the last part, or portion, of what is signified by that which precedes
]; or must be prefixed to the word signifying that part, or portion, as in the saying, [in the Kur xcvii. last verse,]
it, until the time of the rising of the dawn
]. (Mughnee.) It is also followed by a mansoob aor., as in
I journeyed until I entered it,
together with the verb being rendered in grammatical analysis by an inf. n. governed in the gen. case by
]: this is one of the cases in which
]: and in the same sense it is used in the phrase, [in the Kur xx. 93,]
, denoting a cause or motive [of action &c., signifying
To the end that, in order that,
Become a Muslim, to the end that,
so that, thou mayest enter Paradise
]; being in this case, likewise, followed by a mansoob aor. (Mughnee.) ―         -b4-  It is also used [as a preposition virtually governing the gen. case,
being understood after it,] in the sense of
likewise followed by a mansoob aor., as in the following verse:

layosa AlEaTaA='u mina AlfuDuwli samaAHapF
                      
                     
                        Hat~aY tajuwda wamaA ladayoka qaliylu
The giving of superfluities is not liberality:
thou be bountiful when little is in thy possession
]. (Mughnee.) ―         -b5-  It is also a conjunction, like

] (S, Mughnee,) but on three conditions: first, that the word following it and conjoined by it be a noun properly so called, not a pronoun: secondly, that this noun signify a part, or portion, of what is signified by that which precedes
The pilgrims arrived: even those on foot
I ate the fish: even its head
]: thirdly, that the noun following it and conjoined by it denote either the greatest or the least [literally or figuratively] of what are included in the signification of the noun that precedes
Men have died: even the prophets
The people visited thee: even the cuppers
]. (Mughnee.) ―         -b6-  It is also used as an inceptive particle, (S, Mughnee,) preceding a nominal proposition, (Mughnee,) as in the following verse (of Jereer [so in a copy of the S]):

famaA zaAlati AlqatolaY tamuj~a dimaA='ahaA
                      
                     
                        bidijolapa Hat~aY maA='u dijolapa A^a$okalu
And the slain ceased not to emit their blood into the Tigris, so that the water of the Tigris
of a mixed colour consisting of red and white
]: (S, Mughnee:) and preceding a verbal proposition, of which the verb is a pret., as in the phrase, [in the Kur vii. 93,]
So that they became numerous, and said
]: (Mughnee:) and preceding a marfooa aor., as in the phrase, [in the Kur ii. 210,]
; (IHsh, in De Sacy's “ Anthol. Gramm. Ar., ” p. 82 of the Arabic text;) others reading

, and those in which the marfooa aor. is used, the following observations are made.] When
precedes a future, the latter is mansoob, by reason of

I journeyed to El-Koofeh until I entered it:
see above]: (S:) it is not mansoob unless the verb is a future: if it is future with respect to the time of speaking, it must be mansoob, as in
[cited above]: if the verb is future with respect to what precedes, only, it may be mansoob, as in
